what: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: level grouping column (e.g., insurance type), compute acceptance rates, confidence intervals, disparity metrics relative reference group.

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
code: mysterycall_disparities_table(   data,   outcome_col,   group_col,   ref_group = NULL,   ci_method = c("wilson", "exact", "wald"),   alpha = 0.05 )

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: data data.frame containing study data. outcome_col Character scalar. Name binary outcome column (values must 0/1; NAs dropped calculations). group_col Character scalar. Name grouping column. ref_group Character scalar NULL. Reference group label. NULL, uses first factor level group_col factor, otherwise alphabetically first level. ci_method One "wilson" (default), "exact", "wald". alpha Numeric (0, 1). Significance level. Default 0.05 yields 95% CIs.

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: data.frame class c("mysterycall_disparities_table", "data.frame") sorted reference group first, remaining groups descending acceptance rate. Attributes ref_group, ci_method, alpha attached. Columns: group Group label. n Total observations group. n_accepted Sum outcome_col (excluding NAs). rate Acceptance rate (n_accepted / n). lower_ci Lower bound rate CI. upper_ci Upper bound rate CI. abs_diff Difference percentage points ref group (0 reference). rel_risk Rate relative ref group rate (1 reference). rr_lower Lower bound RR CI via log method (NA reference). rr_upper Upper bound RR CI via log method (NA reference). p_value Two-proportion z-test p-value vs. reference (NA reference). p_value_fmt Formatted p-value string.

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
text: Relative-risk CIs use log method 0.5 continuity correction n_accepted 0: \(SE(\log RR) = \sqrt{1/n_+ - 1/n + 1/n_{ref,+} - 1/n_{ref}}\).

title: Compute Disparity Metrics Across Groups — mysterycall_disparities_table
code: set.seed(1) df <- data.frame(   insurance = sample(c("Medicaid", "Private", "Medicare"), 120, replace = TRUE),   accepted  = rbinom(120, 1, 0.5) ) mysterycall_disparities_table(df, "accepted", "insurance") #> Disparity table -- 3 groups | ref: 'Medicaid' | wilson 95% CI #> Group                       n  n_acc     Rate  95% CI            Abs.Diff  RR (95% CI)             p-value #> ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  #> Medicaid                   38     18    47.4%  32.5%-62.7%          (ref)  1.00 (ref)              (ref) #> Private                    44     21    47.7%  33.8%-62.1%        +0.4 pp  1.01 (0.64-1.59)        0.974 #> Medicare                   38     18    47.4%  32.5%-62.7%        +0.0 pp  1.00 (0.62-1.61)        1.000

what: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
text: Calls mysterycall_poisson_power() across range incidence rate ratios returns ggplot2 line chart showing required sample size per arm changes effect size. Useful Figure 1 mystery-caller manuscript.

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
code: mysterycall_equation_figure(   lambda0 = 14,   irr_seq = seq(1.1, 2, by = 0.05),   alpha = 0.05,   power = 0.8,   both_arms = TRUE )

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
text: lambda0 Numeric. Baseline expected count reference arm (e.g. mean business days reference insurance). Default 14. irr_seq Numeric vector IRR values evaluate. Default seq(1.10, 2.0, = 0.05). alpha Numeric. Two-sided type error rate. Default 0.05. power Numeric. Desired statistical power. Default 0.80. both_arms Logical. TRUE (default), arm receives n patients; total study size 2n.

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure
text: ggplot object. x-axis IRR, y-axis required sample size per arm, vertical dashed line marks IRR = 1 (effect).

title: Poisson power curve: required sample size across a range of IRRs — mysterycall_equation_figure

CONSORT-style inclusion/exclusion flowchart — mysterycall_flowchart
text: Produces top--bottom DiagrammeR DOT flow diagram showing sequential inclusion exclusion providers analysis. step labelled box count; optional exclusion side-boxes branch right. Requires DiagrammeR package (listed Suggests).

title: CONSORT-style inclusion/exclusion flowchart — mysterycall_flowchart

title: CONSORT-style inclusion/exclusion flowchart — mysterycall_flowchart
text: steps Named character vector (named list coercible character). name step label value formatted count display. Steps rendered top--bottom order supplied. Example:   exclusions Optional named character vector. Names must match names steps (step exclusion occurred); values exclusion description displayed side box. Example:   title Optional character scalar placed diagram. NULL (default) adds title node. node_width Numeric. Fixed width flow box inches. Default 3.5. font_size Integer. Base font size node labels. Default 10L.
code: c(   "Physicians in AAOS Directory" = "671",   "Functioning Phone Number"     = "501",   "Successfully Called"          = "432" ) c("Functioning Phone Number" = "170 excluded: no functioning phone")

title: CONSORT-style inclusion/exclusion flowchart — mysterycall_flowchart
text: DiagrammeR / htmlwidget object. Print view RStudio Viewer browser; use DiagrammeRsvg::export_svg() + rsvg::rsvg_pdf() save PDF.

title: CONSORT-style inclusion/exclusion flowchart — mysterycall_flowchart
code:
mysterycall_flowchart(   steps = c(     "Physicians in Directory" = "671",     "Functioning Phone"       = "501",     "Both Insurance Types"    = "432",     "Final Analysis"          = "389"   ),   exclusions = c(     "Functioning Phone"    = "170 excluded: no functioning phone",     "Both Insurance Types" = "69 excluded: Medicaid not accepted",     "Final Analysis"       = "43 excluded: incomplete call data"   ) )  {"x":{"diagram":"digraph flowchart {
  graph [layout=dot, rankdir=TB, overlap=false, fontsize=10]
  node [fixedsize=false]

  NPhysicians_in_Directory [label="Physicians in Directory\nn = 671", shape=box, width=3.5, fontsize=10]
  NFunctioning_Phone [label="Functioning Phone\nn = 501", shape=box, width=3.5, fontsize=10]
  NBoth_Insurance_Types [label="Both Insurance Types\nn = 432", shape=box, width=3.5, fontsize=10]
  NFinal_Analysis [label="Final Analysis\nn = 389", shape=box, width=3.5, fontsize=10]
  EFunctioning_Phone [label="170 excluded: no functioning phone", shape=box, style=dashed, width=3.0, fontsize=10]
  EBoth_Insurance_Types [label="69 excluded: Medicaid not accepted", shape=box, style=dashed, width=3.0, fontsize=10]
  EFinal_Analysis [label="43 excluded: incomplete call data", shape=box, style=dashed, width=3.0, fontsize=10]

  NPhysicians_in_Directory -> NFunctioning_Phone
  NPhysicians_in_Directory -> EFunctioning_Phone [style=dashed, constraint=false]
  NFunctioning_Phone -> NBoth_Insurance_Types
  NFunctioning_Phone -> EBoth_Insurance_Types [style=dashed, constraint=false]
  NBoth_Insurance_Types -> NFinal_Analysis
  NBoth_Insurance_Types -> EFinal_Analysis [style=dashed, constraint=false]
}
","config":{"engine":"dot","options":null}},"evals":[],"jsHooks":[]}

what: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
text: Estimates physician's current age : $$\text{age} = (\text{ref\_year} - \text{grad\_year}) + \text{age\_offset}$$ age_offset (default 27) median age US medical school graduation. method validated Doximity birth-year data 32,998 OB/GYN physicians (r = 0.962, MAE = 2.1 years) 348 ENT physicians (r = 0.957, MAE = 1.7 years); see technical appendix.

title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
code: mysterycall_impute_age(   grad_year,   ref_year = as.integer(format(Sys.Date(), "%Y")),   age_offset = 27L,   min_age = 25,   max_age = 90 )

title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
text: grad_year Integer numeric vector medical school graduation years (4-digit, e.g. 1995). NA values produce NA output. ref_year Integer scalar. Reference year calculation. Defaults current calendar year (.integer(format(Sys.Date(), "%Y"))). age_offset Integer scalar. Assumed age graduation. Default 27L (validated median US physicians). min_age Numeric. Minimum plausible physician age. Values threshold replaced NA. Default 25. max_age Numeric. Maximum plausible physician age. Values threshold replaced NA. Default 90.

title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
text: Integer vector length grad_year.

title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
text: Implausible results (age < 0 computing, age < min_age) set NA warning.

title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
text: Muffly T, et al. Validation Physician Age Imputation Method (Graduation Year + 27). Technical Appendix, 2026.

title: Impute physician age from medical school graduation year — mysterycall_impute_age
code: # Physician who graduated in 1995; current year 2026 → age ≈ 58 mysterycall_impute_age(1995) #> [1] 58  # Vectorized mysterycall_impute_age(c(1980, 1995, 2010, NA)) #> [1] 73 58 43 NA

what: IRR forest plot for a Poisson GLMER result — mysterycall_irr_plot
title: IRR forest plot for a Poisson GLMER result — mysterycall_irr_plot
text: Produces publication-ready forest plot showing fixed-effect term incidence rate ratio (point) Wald confidence interval (horizontal bar). intercept row excluded default. vertical reference line marks IRR = 1 (effect). Requires ggplot2, already package Imports.

title: IRR forest plot for a Poisson GLMER result — mysterycall_irr_plot
code: mysterycall_irr_plot(   x,   include_intercept = FALSE,   reference_line = 1,   point_size = 3,   color_sig = "#C0392B",   color_ns = "#2C3E50",   x_label = "Incidence Rate Ratio (IRR)",   title = NULL,   x_log = FALSE )

title: IRR forest plot for a Poisson GLMER result — mysterycall_irr_plot
text: x mysterycall_poisson_model object mysterycall_poisson_model(), data frame columns term, irr, ci_lower, ci_upper (optionally p_value). include_intercept Logical. FALSE (default) (Intercept) row dropped plotting. reference_line Numeric. X-axis position null-effect vertical line. Default 1 (IRR = 1). point_size Numeric. Size IRR point. Default 3. color_sig Character. Colour terms p_value < 0.05. Default "#C0392B" (red). applied p_value column present. color_ns Character. Colour non-significant terms (p_value absent). Default "#2C3E50" (dark navy). x_label Character. X-axis label. Default "Incidence Rate Ratio (IRR)". title Character. Plot title. NULL (default) produces title. x_log Logical. TRUE X axis log-transformed equal-ratio distances look equal. Default FALSE.

title: IRR forest plot for a Poisson GLMER result — mysterycall_irr_plot
text: ggplot object. Print save ggplot2::ggsave().

title: IRR forest plot for a Poisson GLMER result — mysterycall_irr_plot
code: if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1978)   df <- data.frame(     wait_days = rpois(40, lambda = 18),     insurance = rep(c("Medicaid", "BCBS"), each = 20),     gender    = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 40, replace = TRUE),     physician = rep(paste0("Dr_", 1:8), each = 5),     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   )   result <- suppressWarnings(mysterycall_poisson_model(     df, "wait_days", c("insurance", "gender"), "physician"   ))   mysterycall_irr_plot(result) } #> Fitting Poisson GLMER: wait_days ~ insurance + gender + (1 | physician) #> boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular') #> Model fitted: n=40, physicians=8, AIC=230.8, overdispersion=0.90 #> `height` was translated to `width`.

what: Validate NPI numbers using the official CMS Luhn checksum — mysterycall_luhn_check
title: Validate NPI numbers using the official CMS Luhn checksum — mysterycall_luhn_check
text: Implements CMS-specified Luhn algorithm 10-digit National Provider Identifiers (NPI). CMS prepends constant "80840" NPI computing checksum, generic Luhn check.

title: Validate NPI numbers using the official CMS Luhn checksum — mysterycall_luhn_check
code: mysterycall_luhn_check(npi)

title: Validate NPI numbers using the official CMS Luhn checksum — mysterycall_luhn_check
text: npi Character numeric vector NPI values. Non-10-digit values (coercing character) immediately invalid.

title: Validate NPI numbers using the official CMS Luhn checksum — mysterycall_luhn_check
text: Logical vector length npi. TRUE means NPI passes CMS Luhn checksum; FALSE means malformed. NA inputs return FALSE.

title: Validate NPI numbers using the official CMS Luhn checksum — mysterycall_luhn_check
code: mysterycall_luhn_check(c("1234567893", "9999999999", NA)) #> [1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE # Filter a data frame to valid NPIs: # df[mysterycall_luhn_check(df$npi), ]

what: Choropleth map of appointment acceptance rates by US state — mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate
title: Choropleth map of appointment acceptance rates by US state — mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate
text: Draws choropleth map appointment acceptance rates using base-R map data (external spatial packages required state-level maps).

title: Choropleth map of appointment acceptance rates by US state — mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate
code: mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate(   data,   region_col,   rate_col,   region_type = c("state", "hrr"),   palette = "viridis",   title = "Appointment Acceptance Rate (%)",   legend_title = "Acceptance\nRate (%)",   limits = NULL,   na_color = "grey80",   save_path = NULL,   width = 10,   height = 7,   dpi = 300L )

title: Choropleth map of appointment acceptance rates by US state — mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate
text: data data frame least two columns: region_col rate_col. region_col Character scalar. Name column identifies regions. region_type = "state" can full state names (e.g. "Colorado") standard two-letter abbreviations (e.g. "CO"). rate_col Character scalar. Name numeric column acceptance rates 0 1. Values multiplied 100 display. region_type Character scalar. Either "state" (default) "hrr". "hrr" immediately raises error instructions use sf-based workflow. palette Character scalar. Viridis colour-scale option passed ggplot2::scale_fill_viridis_c(). Default "viridis". title Character scalar. Plot title. Default "Appointment Acceptance Rate (%)". legend_title Character scalar. Legend title. Default "Acceptance\\nRate (%)". limits Numeric vector length 2 NULL. Explicit limits fill scale. NULL (default) lets ggplot2 choose. na_color Character scalar. Fill colour states data. Default "grey80". save_path Character scalar NULL. NULL, plot written path via mysterycall_save_plot(). width Numeric. Width inches passed mysterycall_save_plot(). Default 10. height Numeric. Height inches passed mysterycall_save_plot(). Default 7. dpi Integer. Resolution passed mysterycall_save_plot(). Default 300L.

title: Choropleth map of appointment acceptance rates by US state — mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate
text: ggplot object, invisibly.

title: Choropleth map of appointment acceptance rates by US state — mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate
code: set.seed(7) df <- data.frame(   state = c("Colorado", "California", "Texas", "New York", "Florida"),   rate  = c(0.55, 0.72, 0.48, 0.63, 0.81) ) mysterycall_map_acceptance_rate(df, region_col = "state", rate_col = "rate") #> Error in ggplot2::map_data("state"): The package "maps" is required for `map_data()`.

what: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: Computes average marginal effects predictor fitted Poisson GLM (glm family = poisson) Poisson GLMER (glmerMod lme4). Also accepts mysterycall_poisson_model object, fitted model extracted automatically.

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
code: mysterycall_marginal_effects(   model,   term = NULL,   data = NULL,   type = c("response", "link"),   eps = 1e-07 )

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: model fitted glm (family = poisson), glmerMod object lme4, mysterycall_poisson_model object. mysterycall_poisson_model, $model component extracted automatically. term Character vector predictor names compute marginal effects. Pass variable name (e.g. "cyl"), dummy-encoded column name (e.g. "factor(cyl)6"). NULL (default), effects computed predictors model frame. data Optional data frame use instead stored model data. Columns must use original (un-transformed) variable names. NULL (default), data used fit model retrieved automatically. type Character scalar, either "response" (default) "link". Passed stats::predict(). lme4 models, population-average predictions (re.form = NA) always used. eps Positive numeric scalar. Step size numerical differentiation continuous predictors. Default 1e-7.

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: data.frame one row per term–level combination columns: term Character. predictor name (supplied term derived model frame). level Character. NA continuous predictors; factor level name categorical predictors. ame Numeric. average marginal effect. variable_type Character. Either "continuous" "categorical".

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: continuous predictors, AME estimated numerical differentiation: predicted values computed predictor bumped eps, average finite-difference derivative returned. factor (categorical) predictors, AME non-reference level average difference predicted values level reference level, holding predictors constant. Standard errors confidence intervals computed function. Use mysterycall_bootstrap_ci() bootstrap-based confidence intervals.

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: function returns AME point estimate. Standard errors confidence intervals require bootstrap procedure; see mysterycall_bootstrap_ci().

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: glmerMod objects, predictions use re.form = NA marginal effect averaged population (conditional estimated random effects). lme4 package must installed.

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
text: formula contains -line calls factor(cyl), pass underlying variable name ("cyl") term, transformed name. function resolves original column automatically.

title: Compute average marginal effects for a Poisson GLM or GLMER — mysterycall_marginal_effects
code: m <- glm(vs ~ wt + factor(cyl), data = mtcars, family = poisson()) mysterycall_marginal_effects(m) #>   term level        ame variable_type #> 1   wt  <NA>  0.1560597    continuous #> 2  cyl     6 -0.6971847   categorical #> 3  cyl     8 -1.3230068   categorical mysterycall_marginal_effects(m, term = "wt") #>   term level       ame variable_type #> 1   wt  <NA> 0.1560597    continuous mysterycall_marginal_effects(m, term = "cyl") #>   term level        ame variable_type #> 1  cyl     6 -0.6971847   categorical #> 2  cyl     8 -1.3230068   categorical

what: Generate a boilerplate methods paragraph for a mystery-caller study — mysterycall_methods_paragraph
title: Generate a boilerplate methods paragraph for a mystery-caller study — mysterycall_methods_paragraph
text: Fills standard methods template describing mystery-caller audit methodology, sample size, geographic scope, insurance types tested, outcome measure, analysis software.

title: Generate a boilerplate methods paragraph for a mystery-caller study — mysterycall_methods_paragraph
code: mysterycall_methods_paragraph(   n_physicians,   n_cities,   specialties,   insurance_types = c("Medicaid", "commercial insurance"),   outcome = "business days until a new-patient appointment",   software = "R (R Foundation for Statistical Computing)" )

title: Generate a boilerplate methods paragraph for a mystery-caller study — mysterycall_methods_paragraph
text: n_physicians Integer. Total number physicians contacted. n_cities Integer. Number cities/states covered. specialties Character vector medical specialties included. insurance_types Character vector insurance types tested. Default c("Medicaid", "commercial insurance"). outcome Character scalar describing primary outcome. Default "business days new-patient appointment". software Character scalar naming analysis software. Default "R (R Foundation Statistical Computing)".

title: Generate a boilerplate methods paragraph for a mystery-caller study — mysterycall_methods_paragraph
text: single character string containing methods paragraph.

title: Generate a boilerplate methods paragraph for a mystery-caller study — mysterycall_methods_paragraph
code: mysterycall_methods_paragraph(   n_physicians  = 369,   n_cities      = 10,   specialties   = c("otolaryngology", "neurotology"),   insurance_types = c("Medicaid", "Blue Cross Blue Shield") ) #> [1] "A mystery-caller audit methodology was employed. A total of 369 physicians representing otolaryngology and neurotology were identified from publicly available directories across 10 cities and states in the United States. Mystery callers posed as new patients insured with Medicaid and Blue Cross Blue Shield and contacted each physician's office to request the earliest available new-patient appointment. Calls were standardized and completed within one week of each other. The primary outcome was business days until a new-patient appointment. All analyses were performed using R (R Foundation for Statistical Computing)."

what: Word-ready IRR table from a fitted Poisson model — mysterycall_model_table

title: Word-ready IRR table from a fitted Poisson model — mysterycall_model_table
text: x mysterycall_poisson_model object mysterycall_poisson_model(). include_intercept Logical. FALSE (default) (Intercept) row dropped. digits Integer. Decimal places IRR CI values. Default 2. irr_col Character. Name combined "IRR (95% CI)" column. Default "IRR (95% CI)". p_col Character. Name p-value column. Default "p-value". term_col Character. Name term column. Default "Term".

title: Word-ready IRR table from a fitted Poisson model — mysterycall_model_table

title: Word-ready IRR table from a fitted Poisson model — mysterycall_model_table
code: if (requireNamespace("lme4", quietly = TRUE)) {   set.seed(1978)   df <- data.frame(     wait_days = rpois(40, lambda = 18),     insurance = rep(c("Medicaid", "BCBS"), each = 20),     physician = rep(paste0("Dr_", 1:8), each = 5),     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   )   result <- suppressWarnings(mysterycall_poisson_model(     df, "wait_days", "insurance", "physician"   ))   mysterycall_model_table(result) } #> Fitting Poisson GLMER: wait_days ~ insurance + (1 | physician) #> boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular') #> Model fitted: n=40, physicians=8, AIC=229.1, overdispersion=0.89 #>                Term     IRR (95% CI) p-value #> 1 insuranceMedicaid 1.01 (0.87-1.17)   0.883

what: Generate a Summary Sentence for the Most Common Gender, Specialty, Training, and Academic Affiliation — mysterycall_most_common_gender

text: function calculates returns sentence describes common gender, specialty, training, academic affiliation provided dataset.

title: Generate a Summary Sentence for the Most Common Gender, Specialty, Training, and Academic Affiliation — mysterycall_most_common_gender
text: data data frame containing columns gender, specialty, Provider.Credential.Text, academic_affiliation.

title: Generate a Summary Sentence for the Most Common Gender, Specialty, Training, and Academic Affiliation — mysterycall_most_common_gender
text: character string summarizing common gender, specialty, training, academic affiliation along respective proportions.

title: Generate a Summary Sentence for the Most Common Gender, Specialty, Training, and Academic Affiliation — mysterycall_most_common_gender
text: function filters missing values column determining common value. calculates proportion common value relative total non-missing values column.

title: Generate a Summary Sentence for the Most Common Gender, Specialty, Training, and Academic Affiliation — mysterycall_most_common_gender
code: # Example 1: Basic usage with a small dataset data <- data.frame(   gender = c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male"),   specialty = c("Cardiology", "Cardiology", "Neurology", "Cardiology", "Neurology"),   Provider.Credential.Text = c("MD", "MD", "DO", "MD", "DO"),   academic_affiliation = c("Yes", "No", "Yes", "No", "Yes") ) result <- mysterycall_most_common_gender(data) print(result) #> [1] "The most common gender in the dataset was male (60%). The most common specialty was Cardiology (60%). The most common training was MD (60%). The academic affiliation status most frequently occurring was yes (60%)."  # Example 2: Handling missing data df_with_na <- data.frame(   gender = c("Male", NA, "Female", "Male", "Male"),   specialty = c("Cardiology", "Cardiology", "Neurology", NA, "Neurology"),   Provider.Credential.Text = c("MD", "MD", "DO", "MD", "DO"),   academic_affiliation = c("Yes", "No", "Yes", "No", NA) ) result <- mysterycall_most_common_gender(df_with_na) print(result) #> [1] "The most common gender in the dataset was male (75%). The most common specialty was Cardiology (50%). The most common training was MD (60%). The academic affiliation status most frequently occurring was no (50%)."  # Example 3: Different proportions with a larger dataset df_large <- data.frame(   gender = c(rep("Male", 70), rep("Female", 30)),   specialty = c(rep("Cardiology", 50), rep("Neurology", 30), rep("Orthopedics", 20)),   Provider.Credential.Text = c(rep("MD", 60), rep("DO", 40)),   academic_affiliation = c(rep("Yes", 40), rep("No", 60)) ) result <- mysterycall_most_common_gender(df_large) print(result) #> [1] "The most common gender in the dataset was male (70%). The most common specialty was Cardiology (50%). The most common training was MD (60%). The academic affiliation status most frequently occurring was no (60%)."

what: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust
title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust
text: Wraps stats::p.adjust() convenience features: accepts either numeric vector p-values data.frame named p-value column, adds significance stars, returns tidy data.frame.

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust
code: mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(   x,   method = c("bonferroni", "fdr", "holm", "BH", "BY"),   alpha = 0.05,   p_col = NULL,   label_col = NULL )

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust
text: x numeric vector p-values, data.frame containing p-value column identified p_col. method Character. Correction method passed stats::p.adjust(). One "bonferroni", "fdr", "holm", "BH", "". "fdr" alias "BH". alpha Numeric (0, 1). Significance threshold significant column. Default 0.05. p_col Character scalar NULL. x data.frame, name column holding raw p-values. label_col Character scalar NULL. x data.frame, name column use comparison labels.

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust
text: data.frame columns: comparison Label comparison (label_col, row names, sequential integers). p_raw Original (unadjusted) p-value. p_adjusted Adjusted p-value. significant Logical; TRUE p_adjusted < alpha. stars Significance stars: "***" (p<0.001), "**" (p<0.01), "*" (p<0.05), "ns" otherwise.

title: Adjust P-Values for Multiple Comparisons — mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust
code: pvals <- c(0.001, 0.03, 0.2, 0.5, 0.04) mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(pvals, method = "bonferroni") #>   comparison p_raw p_adjusted significant stars #> 1          1 0.001      0.005        TRUE    ** #> 2          2 0.030      0.150       FALSE    ns #> 3          3 0.200      1.000       FALSE    ns #> 4          4 0.500      1.000       FALSE    ns #> 5          5 0.040      0.200       FALSE    ns  df <- data.frame(   comparison = c("A vs B", "A vs C", "B vs C"),   p          = c(0.01, 0.04, 0.3) ) mysterycall_multiple_comparison_adjust(df, p_col = "p", label_col = "comparison") #>   comparison p_raw p_adjusted significant stars #> 1     A vs B  0.01       0.03        TRUE     * #> 2     A vs C  0.04       0.12       FALSE    ns #> 3     B vs C  0.30       0.90       FALSE    ns

The excluded states include Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, Arkansas, Colorado, Connecticut, Delaware, Florida, Georgia, Hawaii, Idaho, Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Maine, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nebraska, New Hampshire, New Jersey, New Mexico, North Carolina, North Dakota, Ohio, Oklahoma, Oregon, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, South Carolina, South Dakota, Tennessee, Utah, Vermont, Virginia, Washington, West Virginia, Wisconsin, Wyoming and District of Columbia."

title: Calculate and Summarize Physician Age — mysterycall_physician_age
code: # Example 1: Basic usage with a small dataset data <- data.frame(age = c(30, 40, 50, 60, 35, 45, 55, 65)) summary_sentence <- mysterycall_physician_age(data, "age") print(summary_sentence) #> [1] "The median age of the dataset was 47.5 (IQR 25th percentile 38.8 to 75th percentile 56.2)."  # Example 2: Handling missing data df_with_na <- data.frame(age = c(30, 40, NA, 60, 35, NA, 55, 65)) summary_sentence <- mysterycall_physician_age(df_with_na, "age") print(summary_sentence) #> [1] "The median age of the dataset was 47.5 (IQR 25th percentile 36.2 to 75th percentile 58.8)."  # Example 3: Different age distribution df_large <- data.frame(age = c(rep(30, 70), rep(40, 30), rep(50, 20), rep(60, 10))) summary_sentence <- mysterycall_physician_age(df_large, "age") print(summary_sentence) #> [1] "The median age of the dataset was 30 (IQR 25th percentile 30 to 75th percentile 40)."

code: mysterycall_plot_density(   data,   x_var,   fill_var,   x_transform = "none",   dpi = 600,   output_dir = NULL,   file_prefix = "density_plot",   x_label = NULL,   y_label = "Density",   plot_title = NULL,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Create a Density Plot for Mystery Caller Studies with Optional Transformations — mysterycall_plot_density
text: data dataframe containing data plotted. Must contain variables specified x_var fill_var. x_var string representing column name x-axis variable. numeric variable (e.g., waiting time days). fill_var string representing column name fill variable. categorical factor variable (e.g., insurance type). x_transform string specifying transformation x-axis: "log" log transformation (log1p), "sqrt" square root transformation, "none" transformation. Default "none". dpi integer specifying resolution saved plot dots per inch (DPI). Default 600 print-ready outputs. output_dir string representing directory plot files saved. Defaults session-specific folder inside tempdir(). file_prefix string used prefix generated plot filenames. filenames timestamp appended ensure uniqueness. Default "density_plot". x_label string specifying label x-axis. Default NULL (uses x_var). y_label string specifying label y-axis. Default "Density". plot_title string specifying title plot. Default NULL (title). verbose boolean indicating whether print messages saved plot locations. Default TRUE.

title: Plot and Save Estimated Marginal Means (EMMs) — mysterycall_plot_emmeans
text: function computes EMMs using emmeans package, provides adjusted means (summary statistics) levels factors, corrected influence predictors model. particularly useful context mystery caller studies, might want compare outcomes appointment wait times different insurance types, scenarios, demographic groups, controlling potential confounders. plot generated function displays EMMs along 95% confidence intervals, making easy visualize differences groups. plot saved specified directory unique filename includes timestamp.

title: Plot and Save Estimated Marginal Means (EMMs) — mysterycall_plot_emmeans
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() # Example 1: Comparing Appointment Wait Times Between Scenarios # Assume 'model' is a fitted GLM object with appointment wait time as the outcome result <- mysterycall_plot_emmeans(   model_object = model,   specs = "scenario",   variable_of_interest = "scenario",   color_by = "insurance",   output_dir = "Figures/ScenarioComparison" )  # Example 2: Comparing EMMs Across Different Insurance Types # Assume 'model' is a fitted GLM object result <- mysterycall_plot_emmeans(   model_object = model,   specs = "insurance",   variable_of_interest = "insurance",   color_by = "academic",   output_dir = "Figures/InsuranceComparison" )  # Example 3: Visualizing EMMs for Different Age Groups in a Mystery Caller Study # Assume 'model' is a fitted GLM object with age groups as a factor result <- mysterycall_plot_emmeans(   model_object = model,   specs = "age_group",   variable_of_interest = "age_group",   color_by = "gender",   output_dir = "Figures/AgeGroupComparison" )  # Example 4: Interaction Effects Between Gender and Insurance Type # Assume 'model' is a fitted GLM object result <- mysterycall_plot_emmeans(   model_object = model,   specs = c("gender", "insurance"),   variable_of_interest = "gender",   color_by = "insurance",   output_dir = "Figures/InteractionEffects" ) }

title: Full emmeans interaction plot with optional file save — mysterycall_plot_emmeans_full
text: model fitted model accepted emmeans::emmeans(). specs Character vector marginal mean specifications. first element x-axis variable; second (present) becomes grouping variable colour / shape. variable Character scalar used x-axis label (underscores replaced spaces first letter capitalised). group_col Optional character scalar naming grouping column. Overrides second element specs supplied. type Character scalar passed emmeans::emmeans() type argument. Use "response" (default) Poisson models values count/rate scale; use "link" log scale. use_color Logical. TRUE (default) uses colour distinguish groups; FALSE uses shapes linetypes greyscale output. save_path Optional character scalar. Full file path (e.g. "figures/emmeans_insurance.png"). non-NULL, mysterycall_save_plot() called automatically. width, height Numeric. Figure dimensions inches passed mysterycall_save_plot(). Defaults 10 x 6. dpi Integer. Resolution saved figure. Default 300L.

what: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
text: Runs multilevel Poisson regression (lme4::glmer) appropriate mystery caller studies physician may called multiple times (e.g. different insurance types). physician identifier modelled random intercept, accounts correlation within physicians. Fixed-effect results returned incidence rate ratios (IRR) Wald confidence intervals.

title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
code: mysterycall_poisson_model(   data,   outcome,   predictors,   random_intercept,   conf_level = 0.95,   nAGQ = 0L,   offset_col = NULL,   ... )

title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
text: data data frame containing model columns. Rows NA model column dropped fitting; count reported. outcome Character scalar naming count-outcome column (e.g. "business_days_until_appointment"). Must non-negative numeric. predictors Character vector fixed-effect predictor column names. Factor character columns used -; reference level first level alphabetically (first levels() factors). random_intercept Character scalar naming grouping column random intercept (e.g. "name" physician). (1 | column) term added formula automatically. conf_level Confidence level Wald CIs. Default 0.95. nAGQ Integer passed lme4::glmer(). 0 (default) uses fastest approximation; 1 uses Laplace approximation; values > 1 use adaptive Gauss-Hermite quadrature (slower, accurate small cluster sizes). offset_col Optional character scalar naming numeric column use log-offset (e.g. log(exposure time)). supplied, term offset(log(offset_col)) appended fixed-effects formula. ... Additional arguments forwarded lme4::glmer().

title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
text: list class mysterycall_poisson_model containing: model fitted glmerMod object. irr_table Tibble one row per fixed-effect term: term, estimate (log scale), se, z_value, p_value, p_value_fmt (character), irr, ci_lower, ci_upper. random_effects Data frame lme4::VarCorr() describing random-intercept variance standard deviation. factor_refs Named list reference levels character/factor predictors. formula formula passed glmer. n Number complete-case rows used fitting. n_dropped Rows excluded due missing values. n_clusters Number unique values random_intercept. overdispersion Pearson chi-square / residual df. Values substantially 1 suggest overdispersion; consider negative binomial model. convergence List converged (logical), singular (logical), messages (character). aic, bic Model information criteria.

title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
text: IRR 1.40 insurance_typeMedicaid means physicians contacted Medicaid insurance , average, 40% longer wait times reference insurance group. Compute exp(estimate) Wald CI exp(estimate ± z * se).

title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
text: Poisson assumes mean = variance. overdispersion substantially greater 1 (common threshold 2), standard errors underestimated. Consider fitting negative binomial model using quasi-Poisson standard errors inference.

title: Fit a Poisson GLMER for mystery caller wait-time analysis — mysterycall_poisson_model
code: set.seed(1978) df <- data.frame(   wait_days = rpois(40, lambda = 18),   insurance = rep(c("Medicaid", "BCBS"), each = 20),   gender    = sample(c("Male", "Female"), 40, replace = TRUE),   physician = rep(paste0("Dr_", 1:8), each = 5),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) result <- mysterycall_poisson_model(   df,   outcome          = "wait_days",   predictors       = c("insurance", "gender"),   random_intercept = "physician" ) #> Fitting Poisson GLMER: wait_days ~ insurance + gender + (1 | physician) #> boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular') #> Warning: Convergence issues detected: #>   boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular') #> Consider simplifying predictors or using nAGQ = 1. #> Warning: Singular fit: random-intercept variance is ~0. The physician-level random effect explains little variation. #> Model fitted: n=40, physicians=8, AIC=230.8, overdispersion=0.90 result$irr_table #> # A tibble: 3 × 9 #>   term       estimate     se z_value p_value p_value_fmt   irr ci_lower ci_upper #>   <chr>         <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>   <dbl> <chr>       <dbl>    <dbl>    <dbl> #> 1 (Intercep…   2.88   0.0670  43.0     0     <0.001      17.8    15.6      20.3  #> 2 insurance…   0.0109 0.0738   0.148   0.883 0.883        1.01    0.875     1.17 #> 3 genderMale   0.0458 0.0744   0.616   0.538 0.538        1.05    0.905     1.21

what: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
title: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
text: Computes number providers per insurance arm needed detect specified Incidence Rate Ratio (IRR) mystery caller study Poisson count outcome (e.g. business days appointment). provider called per insurance arm.

title: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
code: mysterycall_poisson_power(   irr,   lambda_ref,   alpha = 0.05,   power = 0.8,   both_arms = TRUE,   icc = 0,   calls_per_cluster = 2L )

title: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
text: irr Numeric. minimum detectable incidence rate ratio. Must positive equal 1. lambda_ref Numeric. Expected mean wait time (days) reference insurance group (e.g. BCBS). Must positive. alpha Numeric. Two-sided type error rate. Default 0.05. power Numeric. Desired statistical power (1 - beta). Default 0.80. both_arms Logical. TRUE, provider called insurance types (paired design), halving total providers needed. Default TRUE. icc Numeric [0, 1). Intra-cluster correlation providers clustered (e.g. practice). Applied design-effect inflation: n * (1 + (m - 1) * icc) m calls_per_cluster. Default 0 (clustering adjustment). calls_per_cluster Integer. Average calls per cluster, used icc > 0. Default 2L.

title: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
text: named list elements: n_per_arm Providers needed per insurance arm. n_total Total providers needed. n_total_calls Total calls placed (= n_per_arm * 2 n_total * 2 paired design). irr, lambda_ref, lambda_trt Input parameters plus derived treatment-arm mean. alpha, power Input parameters. design_effect Inflation factor icc > 0.

title: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
text: sample-size formula derived score test comparing two independent Poisson rates \(\lambda_0\) (reference arm, e.g. BCBS) \(\lambda_1 = \text{IRR} \times \lambda_0\) (treatment arm, e.g. Medicaid): $$n = \frac{(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2 \left(\frac{1}{\lambda_0} + \frac{1}{\lambda_1}\right)}{(\log \text{IRR})^2}$$ gives providers per arm. Total providers = n * 2. provider called insurance types (both_arms = TRUE), n providers needed total.

title: Sample-size calculation for a Poisson mystery caller study — mysterycall_poisson_power
code: # Detect IRR = 1.40 (Medicaid 40% longer waits) vs BCBS mean of 14 days mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = 1.40, lambda_ref = 14) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.40, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=19.6 | n_per_arm=9, n_total=9, calls=18 #> $n_per_arm #> [1] 9 #>  #> $n_total #> [1] 9 #>  #> $n_total_calls #> [1] 18 #>  #> $irr #> [1] 1.4 #>  #> $lambda_ref #> [1] 14 #>  #> $lambda_trt #> [1] 19.6 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $power #> [1] 0.8 #>  #> $design_effect #> [1] 1 #>   # Higher power mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = 1.40, lambda_ref = 14, power = 0.90) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.40, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=19.6 | n_per_arm=12, n_total=12, calls=24 #> $n_per_arm #> [1] 12 #>  #> $n_total #> [1] 12 #>  #> $n_total_calls #> [1] 24 #>  #> $irr #> [1] 1.4 #>  #> $lambda_ref #> [1] 14 #>  #> $lambda_trt #> [1] 19.6 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $power #> [1] 0.9 #>  #> $design_effect #> [1] 1 #>   # Smaller effect (IRR = 1.20) mysterycall_poisson_power(irr = 1.20, lambda_ref = 14) #> Poisson power: IRR=1.20, lambda_ref=14.0, lambda_trt=16.8 | n_per_arm=31, n_total=31, calls=62 #> $n_per_arm #> [1] 31 #>  #> $n_total #> [1] 31 #>  #> $n_total_calls #> [1] 62 #>  #> $irr #> [1] 1.2 #>  #> $lambda_ref #> [1] 14 #>  #> $lambda_trt #> [1] 16.8 #>  #> $alpha #> [1] 0.05 #>  #> $power #> [1] 0.8 #>  #> $design_effect #> [1] 1 #>

title: Run comprehensive preflight checks before workflow — mysterycall_preflight_check
text: Validates requirements successful workflow run including API keys, data quality, output directories, estimated resources, dependencies. Provides go/-go decision starting long-running operations.

title: Run comprehensive preflight checks before workflow — mysterycall_preflight_check
code: mysterycall_preflight_check(   input_data,   output_dir,   google_maps_api_key = NULL,   here_api_key = NULL,   check_apis = TRUE,   estimate_resources = TRUE,   prompt_user = interactive(),   interactive = NULL,   required_columns = c("first", "last") )

title: Run comprehensive preflight checks before workflow — mysterycall_preflight_check
text: input_data Either file path (character scalar) CSV/Parquet roster, already-loaded data frame. path, file must exist readable; data frame validated memory. output_dir Character scalar. Destination directory workflow outputs. Created exist. google_maps_api_key Character scalar. Google Maps Platform API key. Required geocoding part workflow; leave NULL skip geocoding validation. here_api_key Character scalar. Routing API key. Required creating drive-time isochrones; leave NULL skip. check_apis Logical. TRUE (default), validates non-NULL API key making minimal test request. Set FALSE skip live validation (e.g., tests offline). estimate_resources Logical. TRUE (default), estimates wall-clock time peak memory consumption run starts. prompt_user Logical. TRUE (default interactive sessions), prints go/-go summary waits user confirmation. Set FALSE non-interactive scripts. interactive Alias prompt_user; non-NULL takes precedence prompt_user. Provided back-compatibility scripts pass interactive = FALSE. required_columns Character vector column names must present input_data. Defaults c("first", "last").

what: Three-tier specialty reconciliation with audit columns — mysterycall_reconcile_specialty
title: Three-tier specialty reconciliation with audit columns — mysterycall_reconcile_specialty
text: Applies three-tier decision rule: Tier 1 (high): primary column non-missing non-default — use -. Tier 2 (medium): primary missing default label, secondary column informative — adopt secondary value. Tier 3 (low): sources uninformative — set specialty default. Two audit columns appended: one recording source used one recording confidence tier.

title: Three-tier specialty reconciliation with audit columns — mysterycall_reconcile_specialty
code: mysterycall_reconcile_specialty(   data,   primary_col,   secondary_col = NULL,   default = "General",   source_col = "specialty_source",   confidence_col = "specialty_confidence" )

title: Three-tier specialty reconciliation with audit columns — mysterycall_reconcile_specialty
text: data data frame. primary_col Character scalar. Column containing primary specialty label (e.g. NPI registry). secondary_col Optional character scalar. Column containing alternative specialty label (e.g. board certification data). default Character scalar. "uninformative" label triggers tier-2 tier-3 logic. Default "General". source_col Character scalar. Name new audit column recording source used. Default "specialty_source". confidence_col Character scalar. Name new audit column recording tier ("high", "medium", "low"). Default "specialty_confidence".

title: Three-tier specialty reconciliation with audit columns — mysterycall_reconcile_specialty
text: data primary_col values possibly updated (tier-2 rows receive secondary value) two new audit columns appended.

title: Three-tier specialty reconciliation with audit columns — mysterycall_reconcile_specialty
code: df <- data.frame(   specialty_npi   = c("Otolaryngology", "General", NA, "Rhinology"),   specialty_abohns = c(NA, "Laryngology", "Rhinology", "General") ) mysterycall_reconcile_specialty(   df,   primary_col   = "specialty_npi",   secondary_col = "specialty_abohns" ) #>    specialty_npi specialty_abohns specialty_source specialty_confidence #> 1 Otolaryngology             <NA>    specialty_npi                 high #> 2    Laryngology      Laryngology specialty_abohns               medium #> 3      Rhinology        Rhinology specialty_abohns               medium #> 4      Rhinology          General    specialty_npi                 high

text: data frame matched columns renamed. Unmatched targets silently skipped; warning issued multiple columns match.

title: Rename columns by substring match — mysterycall_rename_columns
code: df <- data.frame(   doctor_info = 1:5,   patient_contact_data = 6:10 ) # Renaming 'doctor_info' to 'physician_info' df <- mysterycall_rename_columns(df,                                   target_strings = c("doctor"),                                   new_names = c("physician_info")) #> --- Starting to search and rename columns based on target substrings --- #> Matched 1 column(s) by substring match for 'doctor': doctor_info #> Renamed 'doctor_info' to 'physician_info'. #>  #> --- Column renaming complete. Final column set: physician_info, patient_contact_data --- print(df) #>   physician_info patient_contact_data #> 1              1                    6 #> 2              2                    7 #> 3              3                    8 #> 4              4                    9 #> 5              5                   10  # More complex example with multiple renamings df <- data.frame(   doc_information = 1:5,   patient_contact = 6:10,   doctor_notes = 11:15 ) # Renaming 'doc_information' to 'doctor_info' and 'doctor_notes' to 'notes' df <- mysterycall_rename_columns(df,                                   target_strings = c("doc_information", "doctor_notes"),                                   new_names = c("doctor_info", "notes")) #> --- Starting to search and rename columns based on target substrings --- #> Matched 1 column(s) by exact match for 'doc_information': doc_information #> Renamed 'doc_information' to 'doctor_info'. #>  #> Matched 1 column(s) by exact match for 'doctor_notes': doctor_notes #> Renamed 'doctor_notes' to 'notes'. #>  #> --- Column renaming complete. Final column set: doctor_info, patient_contact, notes --- print(df) #>   doctor_info patient_contact notes #> 1           1               6    11 #> 2           2               7    12 #> 3           3               8    13 #> 4           4               9    14 #> 5           5              10    15

what: Run the end-to-end mystery caller workflow — mysterycall_run_workflow
title: Run the end-to-end mystery caller workflow — mysterycall_run_workflow
text: helper orchestrates core steps required prepare execute mystery caller campaign. stitches together roster creation, NPI validation, call sheet preparation, workload splitting, Phase 2 hygiene checks teams can focus execution instead plumbing.

title: Run the end-to-end mystery caller workflow — mysterycall_run_workflow
code: mysterycall_run_workflow(   taxonomy_terms = NULL,   name_data = NULL,   phase1_data,   lab_assistant_names,   output_directory,   phase2_data,   phase2_output_directory = output_directory,   quality_check_path,   phase1_output_directory = output_directory,   split_insurance_order = c("Medicaid", "Blue Cross/Blue Shield"),   phase2_required_strings = c(     "physician_information", "able_to_contact_office", "are_we_including",     "reason_for_exclusions", "appointment_date", "number_of_transfers",     "call_time", "hold_time", "notes", "person_completing",     "state", "npi", "name"   ),   phase2_standard_names = c(     "physician_info", "contact_office", "included_in_study",     "exclusion_reasons", "appt_date", "transfer_count",     "call_duration", "hold_duration", "notes", "completed_by",     "state", "npi", "name"   ),   npi_search_args = list(),   all_states = NULL,   taxonomy_states = NULL,   verbose = interactive(),   npi_progress_observer = NULL )

title: Run the end-to-end mystery caller workflow — mysterycall_run_workflow
text: taxonomy_terms Character vector taxonomy descriptions pass mysterycall_search_taxonomy(). Set NULL skip taxonomy-based searches. name_data Optional data frame containing first last columns use mysterycall_search_and_process_npi(). Provide NULL skip name-based searches. phase1_data Data frame holding Phase 1 calling roster information pass mysterycall_clean_phase1(). lab_assistant_names Character vector caller names used splitting cleaned roster via mysterycall_split_and_save(). Must contain least two entries. output_directory Directory mysterycall_split_and_save() write complete per-caller workbooks. phase2_data Data frame file path consumed mysterycall_clean_phase2(). phase2_output_directory Directory Phase 2 exports written. Defaults output_directory. quality_check_path File path mysterycall_save_quality_table() write quality check CSV. phase1_output_directory Directory mysterycall_clean_phase1() write cleaned Phase 1 CSV. Defaults output_directory. split_insurance_order Ordering passed mysterycall_split_and_save()'s insurance_order argument. Defaults c("Medicaid", "Blue Cross/Blue Shield"). phase2_required_strings Character vector substrings used standardizing Phase 2 column names via mysterycall_clean_phase2(). phase2_standard_names Replacement names corresponding phase2_required_strings. npi_search_args Named list additional arguments forwarded mysterycall_search_and_process_npi(). all_states Optional character vector states supply mysterycall_not_contacted_states(). taxonomy_states Optional character vector two-letter state abbreviations passed mysterycall_search_taxonomy() restrict taxonomy searches specific states. Set NULL search states. verbose Logical. TRUE, print stage updates console running workflow. Defaults interactive(). npi_progress_observer Optional callback receives progress updates mysterycall_search_and_process_npi(). invoked payload function's progress_callback argument.

code: mysterycall_search_and_process_npi(   data,   enumeration_type = "ind",   limit = NULL,   country_code = "US",   filter_credentials = c("MD", "DO"),   save_chunk_size = 10,   dest_dir = NULL,   file_format = NULL,   accumulate_path = NULL,   resume = FALSE,   progress_log = NULL,   notify = TRUE,   progress_callback = NULL,   heartbeat_seconds = NULL,   progress_log_format = c("text", "csv") )

title: Search and Process NPI Numbers — mysterycall_search_and_process_npi
text: data data frame columns 'first' 'last' containing names search. enumeration_type enumeration type NPI search (e.g., "ind", "org", ""). Default "ind". limit maximum number search results request name pair. Default 5. country_code Filter "US". filter_credentials character vector containing credentials filter NPI results. Default c("MD", ""). save_chunk_size number results save per chunk. Default 10. dest_dir Destination directory save chunked results. Default NULL (files written). Files written using format specified file_format. file_format Optional override controlling whether chunked accumulated results saved "csv" "parquet". NULL, format defaults CSV unless accumulate_path ends ".parquet". accumulate_path Optional output path accumulates every non-empty result. provided, results appended successful lookup using format inferred file_format file extension. resume Logical. TRUE accumulate_path already exists, names processed previously (based search_term column) skipped. progress_log Optional file path used log progress updates. log file appended created automatically exist. notify Logical. TRUE, play notification sound processing completes (requires optional beepr package). Defaults TRUE. progress_callback Optional function invoked named list time progress updated. list contains fields event, timestamp, search_term, rows, index, total. heartbeat_seconds Optional numeric value specifying frequently (seconds) emit "still processing" heartbeat update iterating names. Set NULL disable heartbeat. progress_log_format Either "text" (default) append human readable lines progress_log "csv" write structured entries columns timestamp, event, search_term, rows, detail.

what: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
text: function searches NPI Database healthcare providers based taxonomy description. NPI API returns 1\,200 records per query. retrieve providers large specialty, supply character vector state abbreviations via states argument; function loop state combine results, effectively bypassing 1\,200-record cap.

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
code: mysterycall_search_taxonomy(   taxonomy_to_search,   states = NULL,   city = NULL,   limit = 1200L,   write_snapshot = TRUE,   snapshot_dir = NULL,   notify = TRUE )

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
text: taxonomy_to_search character vector containing taxonomy description(s) search . states Optional character vector two-letter US state abbreviations (e.g. c("CO", "CA", "NY")). supplied search repeated state providers captured even large specialties exceed 1\,200-record per-query limit. Pass 50 state abbreviations perform complete national search. Defaults NULL (national search, capped limit records). city Optional city name passed npi::npi_search(). limit Maximum records request per API call. Capped 1\,200 NPI API. Defaults 1200L. write_snapshot Logical. TRUE, retrieved data saved .rds file later reference. Defaults TRUE. snapshot_dir Directory snapshot files written write_snapshot TRUE. Defaults session-specific folder inside tempdir() supplied. notify Logical. TRUE, play notification sound processing completes (requires optional beepr package). Defaults TRUE.

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
text: data frame filtered NPI data based specified taxonomy description.

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
text: Inputs: taxonomy_to_search must exact NUCC taxonomy description string (see mysterycall::taxonomy dataset valid values). NPI registry API must reachable; function retries 3 times exponential back-returning empty data frame. Guarantees: Returns zero-row data frame (never NULL) records found. Output rows deduplicated NPI. write_snapshot = TRUE, .rds file written output_dir reproducible re-runs without re-querying live API. Fails : Network unavailable retries (returns empty data frame, error). states contains invalid two-letter abbreviation (silently skipped).

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
text: O(t * p / 200) HTTP requests t = number state batches p = max_records (default 1,200). page request targets < 200 rows. Full national search across 50 states one taxonomy takes ~2–5 min depending registry load. Results cached -memory per session use_cache = TRUE.

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
text: mysterycall_run_workflow() mysterycall_search_and_process_npi() (indirectly via taxonomy lookup)

title: Search NPI Database by Taxonomy — mysterycall_search_taxonomy
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() # National search (limited to 1200 records per taxonomy): go_data <- mysterycall_search_taxonomy("Gynecologic Oncology")  # Full national search by looping over every state: all_states <- c(   "AL","AK","AZ","AR","CA","CO","CT","DE","FL","GA",   "HI","ID","IL","IN","IA","KS","KY","LA","ME","MD",   "MA","MI","MN","MS","MO","MT","NE","NV","NH","NJ",   "NM","NY","NC","ND","OH","OK","OR","PA","RI","SC",   "SD","TN","TX","UT","VT","VA","WA","WV","WI","WY" ) fpmrs_data <- mysterycall_search_taxonomy(   "Female Pelvic Medicine and Reconstructive Surgery",   states = all_states ) }

title: Split data into multiple parts and save each part as separate Excel files — mysterycall_split_and_save
text: data_or_path Either dataframe containing input data path input data file (RDS, CSV, Parquet, XLS/XLSX). output_directory Directory output Excel files saved. lab_assistant_names Vector lab assistant names name output files. seed Seed value randomization (default 1978). complete_file_prefix Prefix complete output file name (default "complete_non_split_version_"). split_file_prefix Prefix split output file name (default empty). recursive_create Logical indicating directories created recursively (default TRUE). insurance_order Vector insurance types ordered priority call scheduling (default c("Medicaid", "Blue Cross/Blue Shield")).

title: Split data into multiple parts and save each part as separate Excel files — mysterycall_split_and_save

title: Split data into multiple parts and save each part as separate Excel files — mysterycall_split_and_save
text: Inputs: lab_assistant_names must \(\geq\) 2 entries; function errors one name provided. seed controls random assignment rows assistants; seed always produces split. output_directory created absent. Guarantees: Every input row appears exactly one assistant's workbook (row duplication, silent omission). Workbooks sorted insurance_order Medicaid rows appear first. combined "callers" workbook written addition individual files. Disaster Prevention: Prevents unequal workload distribution using round-robin row assignment rather random block sampling, can produce highly skewed splits small rosters.

what: Build a Table 1 for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_table1
title: Build a Table 1 for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_table1
text: Produces publication-ready summary table describing physician study characteristics, optionally stratified grouping variable (typically "insurance"). Continuous variables summarised median [IQR] /mean (SD); categorical variables n (%). Statistical tests chosen automatically: Wilcoxon rank-sum Kruskal-Wallis continuous variables, chi-square Fisher's exact categorical.

title: Build a Table 1 for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_table1
code: mysterycall_table1(   data,   covariates,   stratify_by = NULL,   include_overall = TRUE,   cont_stats = c("median_iqr", "mean_sd"),   digits = 1L,   p_value = TRUE,   min_cell = 5L,   variable_labels = NULL )

title: Build a Table 1 for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_table1
text: data data frame, typically output mysterycall_clean_phase1() merged genderize results. covariates Character vector column names include rows. Numeric columns treated continuous; others categorical. stratify_by Optional single column name used stratifying variable (e.g. "insurance"). NULL (default) single Overall column produced hypothesis tests. include_overall Logical. TRUE (default) Overall column included even stratify_by set. cont_stats Character vector controlling statistics shown numeric variables. Valid choices "median_iqr" (default, first) "mean_sd". can supplied show two rows per variable. digits Number decimal places continuous statistics percentages. Default 1. p_value Logical. TRUE (default) stratify_by set, p_value column appended (Wilcoxon/Kruskal-Wallis continuous, chi-square/Fisher's exact categorical). min_cell Minimum expected cell count Fisher's exact test used instead chi-square (2-group categorical ). Default 5. variable_labels Optional named character vector mapping column names display labels, e.g. c(wait_days = "Wait time (days)"). Unlabelled columns use column name.

title: Build a Table 1 for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_table1
text: list class mysterycall_table1 : table Tibble columns variable, level, Overall (include_overall), one column per stratum, p_value (p_value = TRUE stratified). row one statistic one category level. column_ns Named integer vector sample sizes per column. stratify_by name stratifying column, NULL. n Total rows data.

title: Build a Table 1 for mystery caller studies — mysterycall_table1
code: df <- data.frame(   gender    = c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Female"),   academic  = c("University", "Private Practice", "Private Practice",                 "University", "Private Practice", "University"),   wait_days = c(10, 25, 14, 30, 7, 21),   insurance = c("Medicaid", "BCBS", "Medicaid", "BCBS", "Medicaid", "BCBS") ) result <- mysterycall_table1(   df,   covariates   = c("gender", "academic", "wait_days"),   stratify_by  = "insurance" ) result$table #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   variable  level            Overall       `BCBS (N=3)` `Medicaid (N=3)` p_value #>   <chr>     <chr>            <chr>         <chr>        <chr>            <chr>   #> 1 gender    Female           3 (50.0%)     2 (66.7%)    1 (33.3%)        1.000   #> 2 gender    Male             3 (50.0%)     1 (33.3%)    2 (66.7%)        NA      #> 3 academic  Private Practice 3 (50.0%)     1 (33.3%)    2 (66.7%)        1.000   #> 4 academic  University       3 (50.0%)     2 (66.7%)    1 (33.3%)        NA      #> 5 wait_days Median [IQR]     17.5 [11.0-2… 25.0 [23.0-… 10.0 [8.5-12.0]  0.081   #> 6 wait_days Mean (SD)        17.8 (9.0)    25.3 (4.5)   10.3 (3.5)       NA

title: Publication-ready Table 1 via gtsummary — mysterycall_table1_gtsummary

title: Publication-ready Table 1 via gtsummary — mysterycall_table1_gtsummary
text: data data frame. vars Non-empty character vector column names include rows. names must present data. strata_col appears silently removed warning. strata_col Optional single character column name stratify (e.g. "insurance"). provided, Overall column p-values added automatically. label_list Optional named list mapping column names display labels, forwarded gtsummary::tbl_summary(). missing One "" (default), "ifany", "always". what: Most frequent level(s) of a categorical variable with percentage — mysterycall_table_percentages
title: Most frequent level(s) of a categorical variable with percentage — mysterycall_table_percentages
text: Counts level categorical variable, computes share non-missing rows, returns level(s) highest count. multiple levels tie top count, tied levels returned. NA values excluded counts denominator.

title: Most frequent level(s) of a categorical variable with percentage — mysterycall_table_percentages
code: mysterycall_table_percentages(data_frame, variable)

title: Most frequent level(s) of a categorical variable with percentage — mysterycall_table_percentages
text: data_frame data frame containing categorical variable. variable character string giving column name categorical variable within data_frame.

title: Most frequent level(s) of a categorical variable with percentage — mysterycall_table_percentages
text: data frame one row per level tied highest count. Columns: variable , n (count), percent (exact percentage non-missing rows, rounded).

title: Most frequent level(s) of a categorical variable with percentage — mysterycall_table_percentages
code: # "A" is most frequent: returns 1 row data_frame <- data.frame(category = c("A", "B", "A", "C", "A", "B", "B", "A")) mysterycall_table_percentages(data_frame, "category") #>   category n percent #> 1        A 4      50  # Three-way tie: all three returned df_tie <- data.frame(category = c("A", "B", "A", "B", "C", "C", "C", "A", "B")) mysterycall_table_percentages(df_tie, "category") #>   category n  percent #> 1        A 3 33.33333 #> 2        B 3 33.33333 #> 3        C 3 33.33333  # NAs are excluded from counts and the denominator df_na <- data.frame(category = c("A", NA, "A", "C", "A", "B", "B", NA)) mysterycall_table_percentages(df_na, "category") #>   category n percent #> 1        A 3    37.5

title: Calculate the Proportion of Each Level in a Categorical Variable — mysterycall_table_proportion
text: data data frame containing categorical variable. variable_name name categorical variable proportions calculated, passed unquoted expression.

title: Calculate the Proportion of Each Level in a Categorical Variable — mysterycall_table_proportion
text: data frame one row per unique non-missing level variable_name. Columns: variable , n (count), percent (percentage total, rounded 2 decimal places).

title: Calculate the Proportion of Each Level in a Categorical Variable — mysterycall_table_proportion
text: variable_name uses tidy evaluation ({{ }}), pass column name unquoted (e.g. mysterycall_table_proportion(df, gender)). Use mysterycall_table_percentages() column name string.

title: Calculate the Proportion of Each Level in a Categorical Variable — mysterycall_table_proportion
code: # Example 1: Basic usage with a simple dataset data <- data.frame(gender = c("Male", "Female", "Female", "Male", "Male", "Female")) result <- mysterycall_table_proportion(data, gender) print(result) #>   gender n percent #> 1 Female 3      50 #> 2   Male 3      50  # Example 2: Handling a dataset with missing values df_na <- data.frame(gender = c("Male", NA, "Female", "Female", "Male", "Female", NA)) result <- mysterycall_table_proportion(df_na, gender) print(result) #>   gender n percent #> 1 Female 3   42.86 #> 2   Male 2   28.57 #> 3   <NA> 2   28.57  # Example 3: Using a variable with multiple levels df_multi <- data.frame(grade = c("A", "B", "A", "C", "B", "A", "C", "B")) result <- mysterycall_table_proportion(df_multi, grade) print(result) #>   grade n percent #> 1     A 3    37.5 #> 2     B 3    37.5 #> 3     C 2    25.0

what: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
text: function reads CSV file containing NPI numbers, validates format using npi package, removes rows missing invalid NPIs.

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
code: mysterycall_validate_npi(input_data)

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
text: input_data Either data frame containing NPI numbers path CSV file.

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
text: data frame containing valid NPI numbers, invalid missing NPI rows removed.

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
text: Inputs: input_data must contain npi column (character numeric). NPIs validated via Luhn checksum algorithm (NPI standard). Guarantees: Output rows \(\subseteq\) input rows — rows removed, never added. Every removed row either NA, non-10-digit string, failed Luhn checksum. Output NPI column always character type. Disaster Prevention: Prevents silent retention placeholder NPIs (e.g., -zeros test values "1234567890") match real providers downstream joins inflate match rates.

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
text: O(n) small constant; Luhn validation pure arithmetic check. Expect < 0.1 s 10,000 rows.

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
text: mysterycall_run_workflow() mysterycall_search_and_process_npi()

title: Validate and Remove Invalid NPI Numbers — mysterycall_validate_npi
code: if (FALSE) { # interactive() df <- data.frame(npi = c("1234567893", "0000000000", NA_character_)) mysterycall_validate_npi(df) }

title: Verify the content-addressable identity of an audit trail JSON file — mysterycall_verify_artifact
text: Loads audit JSON written mysterycall_clean_phase1(), strips volatile fields, recomputes SHA-256 digest stable payload, compares stored artifact_id. mismatch means file modified written artifact_id computed different logic.

title: Verify the content-addressable identity of an audit trail JSON file — mysterycall_verify_artifact

title: Verify the content-addressable identity of an audit trail JSON file — mysterycall_verify_artifact
text: Invisibly returns TRUE verification passes. Throws informative error verification fails file parsed.

title: Verify the content-addressable identity of an audit trail JSON file — mysterycall_verify_artifact
text: Inputs: audit_path must point readable JSON file produced mysterycall_clean_phase1() schema version >= 1.2.0. Guarantees: Returns TRUE (invisibly) artifact_id matches recomputed digest. outcome raises error diagnostic message. Never modifies audit file. Fails : audit_path exist valid JSON. file produced schema version prior 1.2.0 (artifact_id). recomputed digest match stored artifact_id.

title: Verify the content-addressable identity of an audit trail JSON file — mysterycall_verify_artifact

Wilcoxon rank-sum (Mann-Whitney U): p = 0.885" #>

what: Generate a results paragraph for a mystery caller Poisson model — mysterycall_write_results_paragraph
title: Generate a results paragraph for a mystery caller Poisson model — mysterycall_write_results_paragraph
text: Produces ready--paste results sentence describing IRRs multivariable Poisson regression, formatted clinical manuscripts.

title: Generate a results paragraph for a mystery caller Poisson model — mysterycall_write_results_paragraph
code: mysterycall_write_results_paragraph(   model_result,   ref_group,   exposure_col,   outcome_label = "appointment acceptance",   alpha = 0.05,   irr_digits = 2L,   ci_digits = 2L,   p_digits = 3L )

title: Generate a results paragraph for a mystery caller Poisson model — mysterycall_write_results_paragraph
text: model_result Either mysterycall_poisson_model object (element $irr_table) data frame columns term, irr, ci_lower, ci_upper, p_value. ref_group Character scalar: reference group label (e.g. "commercial insurance"). exposure_col Character scalar: name exposure variable whose terms described (e.g. "insurance"). outcome_label Character scalar: human-readable outcome label. Default "appointment acceptance". alpha Numeric: significance level. Default 0.05. (Currently reserved future use.) irr_digits Integer: decimal places IRR. Default 2L. ci_digits Integer: decimal places CI bounds. Default 2L. p_digits Integer: decimal places p-value. Default 3L.

title: Generate a results paragraph for a mystery caller Poisson model — mysterycall_write_results_paragraph
text: single character string containing result sentences.

title: Generate a results paragraph for a mystery caller Poisson model — mysterycall_write_results_paragraph
code: irr_tbl <- data.frame(   term     = c("(Intercept)", "insuranceMedicaid", "insuranceUninsured"),   irr      = c(1.0, 0.72, 0.55),   ci_lower = c(NA,  0.60, 0.40),   ci_upper = c(NA,  0.86, 0.75),   p_value  = c(NA,  0.0003, 0.0000001),   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) mysterycall_write_results_paragraph(irr_tbl, "commercial insurance", "insurance") #> [1] "In multivariable Poisson regression, insurance was significantly associated with appointment acceptance (see Table X). Compared with commercial insurance, callers presenting as Medicaid had an IRR of 0.72 (95% CI 0.60-0.86; p < 0.001) for appointment acceptance. Compared with commercial insurance, callers presenting as Uninsured had an IRR of 0.55 (95% CI 0.40-0.75; p < 0.001) for appointment acceptance."

what: Normalize All Address Fields in a Data Frame — normalize_address_df

text: Applies comprehensive USPS normalization address fields data frame: ASCII normalization, directional standardization, suffix standardization, unit normalization, state code conversion, ZIP extraction.

title: Normalize All Address Fields in a Data Frame — normalize_address_df
text: df Data frame containing address columns. addr1_col Character name primary address column. Default: "practice_address1". addr2_col Character name secondary address column. Default: "practice_address2". city_col Character name city column. Default: "practice_city". state_col Character name state column. Default: "practice_state". zip_col Character name ZIP code column. Default: "practice_zip".

title: Normalize All Address Fields in a Data Frame — normalize_address_df
text: input data frame original columns modified plus additional derived columns: address1_norm Fully normalized primary address. address2_norm Fully normalized secondary address. address1_no_unit Primary address unit designators removed. is_po_box Logical: PO Box address? has_num Logical: address start street number?

title: Normalize All Address Fields in a Data Frame — normalize_address_df
code: df <- data.frame(   practice_address1 = c("123 North Main Street Suite 100", "456 Oak Avenue"),   practice_address2 = c(NA, "Apartment 4B"),   practice_city     = c("Denver", "Los Angeles"),   practice_state    = c("Colorado", "CA"),   practice_zip      = c("80111-1234", "90210"),   stringsAsFactors  = FALSE ) normalize_address_df(df) #> # A tibble: 2 × 10 #>   practice_address1  practice_address2 practice_city practice_state practice_zip #>   <chr>              <chr>             <chr>         <chr>          <chr>        #> 1 123 North Main St… NA                DENVER        CO             80111        #> 2 456 Oak Avenue     Apartment 4B      LOS ANGELES   CA             90210        #> # ℹ 5 more variables: address1_norm <chr>, address2_norm <chr>, #> #   address1_no_unit <chr>, is_po_box <lgl>, has_num <lgl>

what: Utility helpers for mysterycall workflows — utility_library
title: Utility helpers for mysterycall workflows — utility_library
text: Collection helper functions streamline dependency checking, file management, logging, quality reporting across mysterycall projects. See individual help pages full documentation: mysterycall_check_dependencies — verify optionally   install package dependencies. mysterycall_resolve_path — normalize project-relative paths. mysterycall_check_data_completeness — compute column-level   completeness metrics. mysterycall_export_with_backup — write outputs   timestamped backups. mysterycall_use_quiet_logging — toggle quiet-mode logging. mysterycall_log_info — emit timestamped log message. mysterycall_standard_labels — canonical presentation labels. mysterycall_standard_palette — standard color palettes. mysterycall_quality_tier — map completeness score   quality tier.

title: mysterycall 1.3.0
text: Released 2026-05-08.

title: mysterycall 1.3.0
text: Package renamed tyler mysterycall. library(tyler) longer work; use library(mysterycall). 100 exported functions now carry mysterycall_ prefix (e.g., mysterycall_geocode(), mysterycall_search_and_process_npi()). previous tyler_ prefix names retained deprecated shims emit warning forward new name. even-older unprefixed names (e.g., check_normality()) continue work double-deprecated shims.

title: mysterycall 1.3.0
text: Replaced 34 \dontrun{} blocks @examplesIf interactive(). Replaced 18 live print() calls message() invisible(). Moved sf Imports Suggests; added requireNamespace() guards 8 geospatial functions avoid forcing GDAL/GEOS/PROJ users. Bumped minimum R version 3.5.0 4.1.0. Removed duplicate Maintainer: field DESCRIPTION. Added GitHub issue/PR templates repostatus.org badge.

title: mysterycall 1.2.2
text: Released 2026-05-04.

title: mysterycall 1.2.2
text: library(tyler) longer crashes R causes system memory exhaustion. Seven heavy packages (ggmap, ggspatial, hereR, leaflet, leaflet.extras, lme4, censusapi) moved Imports Suggests compiled spatial libraries (GDAL, GEOS, PROJ) loaded relevant function first called, package attach. Two packages declared Imports never called (tigris, effects) removed entirely. create_isochrones() longer accumulates memoized results RAM indefinitely. internal memoization object now exposed mysterycall_clear_isochrone_cache(). Call processing large batch reclaim memory. create_isochrones_for_dataframe() create_individual_isochrone_plots() previously called beepr::beep() unconditionally even though beepr suggested package. calls now guarded requireNamespace().

title: mysterycall 1.2.2
text: states — loops state deduplicates NPI, bypassing NPI API’s hard 1,200-record-per-query cap. Pass 50 state abbreviations perform complete national search. city — optional city filter passed directly npi::npi_search(). limit — controls records per API call (max 1,200). mapping geospatial functions now emit clear stop() message exact install.packages() command required optional package installed.

title: mysterycall 1.2.1
text: Released 2025-10-23.

title: mysterycall 1.2.1
text: Released align metadata artifacts package website codemeta specification. Introduced Imotive News & Changelog vignette centralising release notes. Documented mysterycall_run_workflow() coordinates roster creation, validation, call preparation, QA Imotive projects.

title: mysterycall 1.2.1
text: mysterycall_not_contacted_states() now ignores rows without affirmative contact outcomes reports number unique physicians reached.

title: mysterycall 1.2.1
text: search_npi() → use mysterycall_search_and_process_npi() test_and_process_isochrones() → use mysterycall_isochrones_for_df() process_and_save_isochrones() → use mysterycall_isochrones_for_df()

title: mysterycall 0.0.0.9000
text: Added NEWS.md track changes. Verified R-CMD-check workflows macOS, Windows, Ubuntu. Moved provider Suggests; added runtime checks throughout. Refactored mysterycall_genderize() use Genderize.io API, removing dependency non-CRAN genderdata package. Added mysterycall_geocode() simplify geocoding lists addresses. Added vignette skeleton aggregating provider data.
